speaker: interviewer
text: December the 6th, 1995. Hanns Saenger. Interviewer, Heather Rushovich. Johannesburg, South Africa. In English. Hanns, just going back to your life in Paris. How did the citizens accept you into their society as-- as German Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the French don't like foreigners to start with. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is not a question of anti-Semitism. But if you're not a Frenchman, you're not accepted. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To give you an example, when I was saying Kaddish for my late mother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and during the period of three years, my neighbors in Cheux never once wished me either Good Shabbos or Gut Yontif or shook my hand once in-- in three years. To them, I was a Bosch, which is a French way of describing a German. And with the exception of the friends that my parents had, we had no French Jewish friends during the time that we lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even at school.

speaker: interviewee
text: At school, yes. I had one Jewish boy. And he and I became friends, and I visited him occasionally. Don't forget that school life in France is very disciplined. It's very intense. It's very hard work. It's probably the best education in the world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it gives you no free time at all. It's highly disciplined. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The school that I went to was called the Lycee Janson de Sailly, which still exists. And it has 3,000 pupils. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] About a thousand of them are borders. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you can understand that life had to be disciplined. And the French school system [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was much more similar to the university life in this country. You-- you were taught how to find your own references in the library. You were taught how to research much earlier than-- than South African schools. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In fact, in the last years of school, before matric, you no longer have a teacher who is in charge of your class. You're being taught by university professors who come and teach their subject. They don't know your name. They take no-- they're not interested in you. Only if you are particularly outstanding and show talent do they take a personal interest. And you're only allowed to write your-- what in French is called composition-- it's your tests, provided you have attended a given number of lectures in that subject since the last test was written. So it was not personal at all. But it's very intense, the education. No sports at all, except I think we went swimming once a week or twice a week. I don't want to exaggerate, but that-- that-- and that was not in the school. We had to go out for that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The exercise consisted of going round the courtyard in the morning after breakfast and in the evening after dinner. And in the free period, we could play a type of baseball game [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the courtyard. But that was basically-- we didn't even have a gym, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] time for gymnastics. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But you got, I think it was, two weekends off a-- a month, provided you had no bad marks against you during the previous period. The first punishment if you did something wrong was if you were late for class, and then your weekend was canceled. And you had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Thursday afternoon-- either Wednesday or Thursday-- Thursday afternoon, there was no school. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that time was spent in the museums. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, Paris is a city of culture, and there's so much to-- to learn and to see that you can't do it, literally, in a lifetime in any depth. And my father found a German-Jewish lawyer who was an illegal immigrant-- couldn't get a carte d'identite-- and who was absolutely penniless. But he was a very proud man, and he wouldn't accept any help. And my dad asked him whether he would take me on Sundays, on the weekends when I was off, and take me through French museums. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this he-- he accepted. And he accepted money for that, and that was one way that my father could at least provide him with some small income. And those museum visits, which were prepared by him in historical sequence, whatever I know today on-- of art or of culture, that's how I learned it. It was invaluable. Unfortunately, this man was-- disappeared during the war. We could never find him. And I presume he was taken back to Germany and to one of the camps. We were not able to find him. The Red Cross couldn't find him. Just disappeared. Of course, we were here by then, and we had to search for him from here. And the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so there was a small German-Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] crowd of immigrants. And my father was occupied with helping them and organizing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like in what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, you had to find places for them. You had to find countries that would take them, because although we had a carte d'identite, which you had to renew every month-- and you had to slip the officialer a-- a tip every month, otherwise, you didn't get the stamp-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this was not for permanent residence in France. France only offered you [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] temporary residence until you could find a country that would accept you. And the world was basically closed to German Jews. I mean, there wasn't a European country that would take Jews--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get into-- into France?

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd let you--

speaker: interviewer
text: They let you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in on these temporary--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the temporary.

speaker: interviewee
text: --temporary permits. But you had to report every movement to the police. I mean, if you traveled somewhere, you had to get a police stamp that you were going on a three-day trip. And it was stamped into your book. You had a little identity book. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the-- it was very difficult to find countries that would accept you. South Africa worked on a-- on a-- on a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] quota system. America wouldn't let-- hard-- would let hardly any in. There was a small quota, but insignificant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Australia took some. Canada took a few. But the bulk of them had to go to Shanghai, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that is how the-- one of the reasons why German Jews suddenly arrived in-- in Shanghai, because it was one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] part of China that took refugees. Now, it was a well-known fact that the-- the governments knew exactly what was going on in Germany. Their ambassadors have reported back. I mean, the Pope, at the time, Pius XII, I think it was, was [INAUDIBLE] in Berlin in 1933. He knew exactly what was happening, but nobody-- nobody-- admitted it. Since the archives of those days are being made public-- I mean, if you read Anthony Eden, if you read Roosevelt, they knew exactly what was going on. I mean, Roosevelt had a secretary of the treasury who was a Jew, Morgenthau, who begged of him to-- to let Jews come into America. He said no. Britain wouldn't take any, except there was-- in 1938, they allowed in the-- what is called the Kindertransport. They allowed children under 16. And I think they took in three-odd thousand. But parents had to say goodbye to them, and were likely not to see them again. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But that's the only organized lot of Jews that went into-- into England. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was no-- nowhere to go for people [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] legally. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the French didn't help, the French Jews. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What did help was that my father had money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's always a different thing when-- when you have money. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, we lived in comfort. We had a-- a beautiful apartment near the Eiffel Tower. My mother had to be operated again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in fact, there was a-- from a humanitarian point of view, she wanted to have the second operation from-- performed by the professor who had done her first operation in Germany. And the French professor who was looking after her said, all right, bring him out. We'll go into the theater together, and when it comes to it, I'll cut the wound open. And then I'll step aside, and he can do the operation. That was, of course, highly illegal. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she had her wish. And then her professor actually operated her and removed her stomach. That's the first time that a stomach-- total stomach was removed from a patient in Europe. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Unfortunately, it was too late and she died [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shortly after that operation. She died in 1934. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] The-- she was very close-- she had two brothers, and she was very close to the older one who was-- he was a dental surgeon. And he used to come and visit us occasionally from Germany for a day or two. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she made him promise her that he would marry [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when she died, and he married his girlfriend. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And whilst we're talking about this uncle, in 1938, when we were already in South Africa, we heard that he had been taken into a concentration camp. And we got the Red Cross to trace him to Dachau. In those days, you could pay the Nazis 1,500 pounds, and buy a Jew out of a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we sent 1,500 round-- pounds to the-- through the Zionist Federation to the British consul in Hamburg, and he brought him out. And he had him put onto a German boat that went to Palestine. And he also had his wife taken from her apartment, and they met on this boat. And they came out in 1938 to Palestine, where he practiced for the rest of his life, until 1959, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as dental surgeon. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, had-- he was one of 13 children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 11 were girls, and two were boys. So my grandfather called the two boys and said, now I can't afford to send more than one to university, when it's the oldest one's choice whether he wants to go to university or not. And my uncle said, yes, he wanted to become a lawyer. So he-- he went to university, and my father had to go into an apprenticeship. And of the 11 sisters, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 10 perished with their families-- that's husbands and children-- in the camps. And I was never able to-- to trace them, because I didn't remember their married names. So I-- I-- I couldn't. But not one came out. So those families all got killed in-- in the concentration camps. My grandfather was actually a boot maker, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he made boots for Polish officers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had 24 workmen who all lived in. And you can imagine the family, therefore, consisted of 13 children, 24 workmen, and my grandfather and my grandmother. And my grandmother cooked for that whole outfit.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was born in '82. So it must have been from '82 to '95. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when he was 14, he left home. My-- my grandfather had found him an apprenticeship, and he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] don't ever come home without a job, or I'll break every bone in your body. And God bless you. And that was how-- how life went on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother came from Alsace. I think she was born in Metz, but she was educated in Budapest [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before she came to Berlin. And my parents met in-- in Berlin, and got married in 1913. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And war broke out in 1914. My father wouldn't have a child during the war, and hence, I was born in 1919, being a-- a year after the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the first World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: First World War, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you were in-- in Paris, were you aware of what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, up to a point. The press was not-- I mean, the media in those days was not like it is today. There was no television. The only radio communication with Germany was Hitler-- Hitler's broadcasts. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But you-- you got far more hearsay that-- how things were particularly deteriorating from-- you know, as time went on. It got worse and worse. But basically, the Jews had a relatively [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] peaceful time from '33 to '37. The real hell started in-- in 1937 until 19-- until the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know the rest of your family, all your uncles and aunts, were-- were in camps at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, only by inference. I mean, we couldn't reach them. We-- nobody knew where they were, so obviously, they had been taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And after you left Paris, you came to South Africa. OK, what happened-- how did you get to South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we be applied to the South African legation, and Eric Louw, who was probably South Africa's most anti-Semitic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] minister, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was the most charming, helpful minister plenipotentiary in France. He-- he was unbelievably helpful. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He said my father must come and open a hat factory. We want secondary industry in South Africa. We need people with money. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he really made every effort to-- to get all the formalities of our immigration [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sorted out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father told him, he says, well, that's fine. But if I start a factory, then I have to bring a foreman out. And he was born in Chelmno. And Louw said, well, where's Chelmno? He said, Chelmno's in Poland. So Louw said, well, we only allow 75 Polish Jews into the country per year, and that quota is full on the 2nd of January of every year, because the Polish Jews that live in South Africa have family. And they make application to bring their family out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So then my dad said, well, look, I'm the foreman. I come from Chelmno. And I'm not going to go 16 days by boat to Africa. And by the time we get there, the immigration office has got a new directory. They had a book where every town in Europe was listed as to where it was. And Lowe checked that book, and Chelmno was not in the book.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, to my dad, that was a great disappointment. He always told me what a wonderful city Chelmno was. Well, it was a little shtetl. And Louw said, well, look, if it's not in the book, then the immigration officer must accept if you say it's Germany. And my dad said, well, I'm not prepared to take that trip, and you have a new edition of your book when I arrive, and then I come back for 16 days.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Eric Louw said, well, one thing I can't get you a-- is a Polish quota. But I know someone in Pretoria who I think, for 35 pounds, can get you a Polish quota. So we sent 35 pounds to some name that I-- I don't remember, and we got the Polish quota for my brother, myself, and my father and came in here as Poles. And where did you come to? Johannesburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Johannesburg. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the first night in Johannesburg, we stayed at the Ritz Hotel in Commissioner Street. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was near the brewery, what was then the brewery.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my dad said, let's go for a walk before dinner. And I remember very clearly we came out, and we saw a white man kick a newspaper vendor, black newspaper vendor, a boy, literally off the pavement with one kick. And nobody even turned a head, and I thought to myself, what sort of a country have I arrived in? I mean, people don't even stop when they see that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was my first impression of what eventually became Apartheid. And that existed in 1936. Nobody turned around. And we stayed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a place called the Rio Grande, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was opposite the brewery, just off Commissioner Street, for about six months. And then we moved into Buckingham Court, which was opposite the Wellman Street shoe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it? A block of flats?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a brand new block of flats. We were the first tenants. And I think we had a two-room-- a three-room flat for 16 guineas, which was the height of luxury as far as South Africa was then concerned. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the technical college to take shorthand typing in order to learn English phonetically, because I was told that English is a language that is not pronounced the way it's written. So I learned it phonetically, because people said to me, you can always learn to write it later. But first you must learn to speak it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took a course of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] English shorthand typing. And the teacher told me, well, whilst you're about it, why don't you also take bookkeeping? It's the same course. So I took bookkeeping as well, but when the result-- when the exam was written, I couldn't balance the balance sheet, so I knew that I'd failed. And a friend of mine said to me, you know, your name is up on the board. The principal wants to see you. And I went to see him at the technical college opposite the station, and he says, why don't you come and collect your bookkeeping certificate? I said, well, how could I have passed? I know that my balance sheet didn't pass-- didn't-- it didn't balance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, well, he'll make inquiries. And he'd phoned me, and he said, please come and see me again. And I went down. And he said, look, your balance sheet was actually quite correctly put-- set out, but you took 20 schillings to the pound-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and 12-- no. 20-- 12 schillings to the pound, and 20 pence to the schilling. And that's why it didn't balance. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I became a qualified bookkeeper, [LAUGHS] despite the mistake. And my first job I found at Norman Anstey's, which was a department store, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where I took-- where I became a window dresser, took a three-year course. Right. OK.
